claim: Quality is the #1 barrier to putting agents in production, ahead of cost, latency, and security according to a LangChain survey of 1000 agent builders (32% cited quality)
actionable: Prioritize evaluation frameworks in your agentic AI workflow before focusing on optimization for cost or latency.
source_title: How to Build Self-Improving AI Agents in 2026

claim: The GPA framework (7 LLM judges) catches 40% more errors than a single LLM judge when evaluating agents
actionable: Use the GPA framework with dimensions like goal fulfillment, answer relevance, logical consistency, and execution efficiency for comprehensive agent evaluation.
source_title: How to Build Self-Improving AI Agents in 2026

claim: DSPy enables zero prompt engineering by automatically generating optimal prompts based on signatures and metrics without manual prompt writing
actionable: Replace manual prompt engineering with DSPy's signature-based approach to automatically optimize prompts for your agent tasks.
source_title: How to Build Self-Improving AI Agents in 2026

claim: MIPROv2 optimizer in DSPy improves agent efficiency by 25-65% based on specific metrics by selecting top-scoring traces and proposing new prompts
actionable: Use DSPy's MIPROv2 optimizer when tuning agent prompts, understanding it measures all traces and selects top performers for prompt generation.
source_title: How to Build Self-Improving AI Agents in 2026

claim: GEPA (UC Berkeley) uses Pareto frontier and genetic algorithms with reflective mutation and system-aware merge to outperform MIPROv2 by 10% using 40x fewer rollouts
actionable: Switch to GEPA for prompt optimization when cost is a concern, as it produces better results with significantly fewer model calls than MIPROv2.
source_title: How to Build Self-Improving AI Agents in 2026

claim: Only 50% of teams doing observability tools use structured evaluations, and only 23% combine offline and online evaluations
actionable: Implement both offline (batch) and online (live) evaluation strategies rather than relying solely on production observability metrics.
source_title: How to Build Self-Improving AI Agents in 2026

claim: There is an 'action gap' between evaluating agents and actually fixing them; most tools evaluate but few automatically improve agents
actionable: Pair evaluation frameworks with automated improvement systems like TEI loop to bridge the gap between identifying and fixing agent failures.
source_title: How to Build Self-Improving AI Agents in 2026

claim: The TEI loop (Target-Evaluate-Improve) divides improvements into structural fixes and prompt optimization, using Pareto frontier methodology like GEPA
actionable: When improving agents, first address structural code issues until they meet quality targets, then move to prompt optimization using Pareto frontier methods.
source_title: How to Build Self-Improving AI Agents in 2026

claim: Running TEI loop with 30 iterations on a customer support agent using Claude Opus costs $11 and takes about 50 minutes
actionable: Budget approximately $11 and 50 minutes for a full optimization cycle when using Claude Opus with the TEI loop on a single agent.
source_title: How to Build Self-Improving AI Agents in 2026

claim: Observability measures latency, tokens, and cost but does not tell you if your agent actually performed its function correctly; evaluation does
actionable: Use both observability tools (for operational metrics) and evaluation frameworks (for functional correctness) to fully assess agent quality.
source_title: How to Build Self-Improving AI Agents in 2026
